fr:title.text: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:title.text: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}} be a relation; for any semiring S, we may form the {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over S associated to R as follows:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let M be an {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring S. We define the relation associated to M below:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The associated matrix function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is a section of the associated relation function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} for any semiring S.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J0/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › jms-00J0/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We must check that \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S = \mathsf {id}_{\mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}. Fixing a relation R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}, we compute:

The other composite \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is not in general the identity function, but is (necessarily) an idempotent on the set of matrices over S. We will see that this idempotent, in some sense, measures the degree to which the base semiring S is not boolean.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The idempotent {\operatorname {\underline {mat}}_\mathbb {B}\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_\mathbb {B}\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}} is in fact the identity function on matrices over the boolean semiring \mathbb {B}.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IX/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › jms-00IX/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We fix a matrix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and compute:
Because \mathbb {B} is the boolean semiring, any scalar s\in S is either 0 or 1. Therefore, we conclude:

Thus we conclude that an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over the booleans is the same thing as a relation from {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: references
context

fr:title.text: relations and matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: relations and matrices

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Relations between finite sets can be desribed in a more computationally friendly way by their tabulation as matrices. In particular, we shall see in § that an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over the boolean semiring is precisely the same thing as a relation from {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}, where {\mathopen {}\left [l\right ]\mathclose {}}={\mathopen {}\left \{i\mid 0\leq i < l\right \}\mathclose {}} is the set of natural numbers strictly smaller than l. Then we will see that relational composition is, under this correspondence, the same as matrix multiplication.

fr:title.text: matrix over a semiring

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix over a semiring

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For natural numbers m and n, an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring {\mathopen {}\left (S,0,\oplus ,1,\odot \right )\mathclose {}} is given by entries M_{i,j}\in S for all i\in {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} and j\in {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}. We will write \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} for the set of {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices.

fr:title.text: matrices as tables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrices as tables

fr:mainmatter.html:p: {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices can be depicted in tables or grids with rows in the first dimension and columns in the second dimension. For example, let M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (3,2\right )\mathclose {}} be the matrix over the booleans defined by the following equation:
Then M is depicted by the following table with three rows and two columns:

fr:title.text: the identity matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the identity matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any m\in \mathbb {N}, we define the identity {\mathopen {}\left (m\times m\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a given semiring S as follows:
The identity matrix is sometimes called the diagonal matrix, for reasons that become apparent when visualising it according to Notation :

fr:title.text: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:title.text: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}} be a relation; for any semiring S, we may form the {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over S associated to R as follows:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let M be an {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring S. We define the relation associated to M below:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The associated matrix function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is a section of the associated relation function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} for any semiring S.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J0/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › jms-00J0/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We must check that \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S = \mathsf {id}_{\mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}. Fixing a relation R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}, we compute:

The other composite \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is not in general the identity function, but is (necessarily) an idempotent on the set of matrices over S. We will see that this idempotent, in some sense, measures the degree to which the base semiring S is not boolean.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The idempotent {\operatorname {\underline {mat}}_\mathbb {B}\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_\mathbb {B}\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}} is in fact the identity function on matrices over the boolean semiring \mathbb {B}.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IX/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › jms-00IX/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We fix a matrix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and compute:
Because \mathbb {B} is the boolean semiring, any scalar s\in S is either 0 or 1. Therefore, we conclude:

Thus we conclude that an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over the booleans is the same thing as a relation from {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: multiplication of matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: multiplication of matrices

fr:title.text: matrix multiplication in geometry

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix multiplication in geometry

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Although we do not explore it in this course, the viewpoint of matrix multiplication as relational composition generalises to a correct explanation of the role of matrices in geometry as presentations of linear maps between vector spaces in terms of an (uncanonical) choice of basis.

fr:title.text: product of matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: product of matrices

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let M be an {\mathopen {}\left (l\times m\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix and let N be a {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix. The product of M and N, written M\otimes N, is the following {\mathopen {}\left (l\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix:

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The product of matrices is associative and has the identity matrix as neutral element.

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of matrix products › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J3/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of matrix products › jms-00J3/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For associativity, fix matrices L\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (k,l\right )\mathclose {}}, M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} to check that {\mathopen {}\left (N\otimes M\right )\mathclose {}}\otimes L = N\otimes {\mathopen {}\left (M\otimes L\right )\mathclose {}}. Below we use the associativity of multiplication, commutativity of addition, and distributivity of multiplication over addition in the semiring S:
For one unit law, we fix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and recall the definition of the identity matrix to check that M\otimes I^m = M.

Unfolding the definition of I^m_{i,j} as given in Definition , we see that the iterated sum above expands to m-1 copies of 0\odot M_{k,j} for various k \not = i and one copy of 1\odot M_{i,j}. Thus, we conclude that M\otimes I^m_{i,j} = M_{i,j} using the absorption and unit laws for multiplication as well as the unit laws for addition in S.
The other unit law follows in an analogous way.

fr:title.text: matrix product is relational composition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Under the correspondence between boolean matrices and finite relations (§ , Lemma ), matrix products of boolean matrices correspond to relational composites. In particular, given M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}, we have:

fr:title.text: matrix product is relational composition › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J2/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition › jms-00J2/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We use the fact that the additive operation of the boolean semiring is disjunction and the multiplicative operation is conjunction:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: backlinks

fr:title.text: matrix product is relational composition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Under the correspondence between boolean matrices and finite relations (§ , Lemma ), matrix products of boolean matrices correspond to relational composites. In particular, given M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}, we have:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition › jms-00J2/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We use the fact that the additive operation of the boolean semiring is disjunction and the multiplicative operation is conjunction:

fr:title.text: relations and matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: relations and matrices

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Relations between finite sets can be desribed in a more computationally friendly way by their tabulation as matrices. In particular, we shall see in § that an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over the boolean semiring is precisely the same thing as a relation from {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}, where {\mathopen {}\left [l\right ]\mathclose {}}={\mathopen {}\left \{i\mid 0\leq i < l\right \}\mathclose {}} is the set of natural numbers strictly smaller than l. Then we will see that relational composition is, under this correspondence, the same as matrix multiplication.

fr:title.text: matrix over a semiring

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix over a semiring

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For natural numbers m and n, an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring {\mathopen {}\left (S,0,\oplus ,1,\odot \right )\mathclose {}} is given by entries M_{i,j}\in S for all i\in {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} and j\in {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}. We will write \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} for the set of {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices.

fr:title.text: matrices as tables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrices as tables

fr:mainmatter.html:p: {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices can be depicted in tables or grids with rows in the first dimension and columns in the second dimension. For example, let M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (3,2\right )\mathclose {}} be the matrix over the booleans defined by the following equation:
Then M is depicted by the following table with three rows and two columns:

fr:title.text: the identity matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the identity matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any m\in \mathbb {N}, we define the identity {\mathopen {}\left (m\times m\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a given semiring S as follows:
The identity matrix is sometimes called the diagonal matrix, for reasons that become apparent when visualising it according to Notation :

fr:title.text: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: correspondence between matrices and finite relations

fr:title.text: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the matrix associated to a finite relation

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}} be a relation; for any semiring S, we may form the {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over S associated to R as follows:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the relation associated to a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let M be an {\mathopen {}\left (n\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring S. We define the relation associated to M below:

We have defined a function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The associated matrix function \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\colon \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} \to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is a section of the associated relation function \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}} for any semiring S.

fr:title.text: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J0/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: a retraction from matrices to finite relations › jms-00J0/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We must check that \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S = \mathsf {id}_{\mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left ({\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}},{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}. Fixing a relation R\colon {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}}\mathbin {\nrightarrow }{\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}, we compute:

The other composite \operatorname {\underline {mat}}_S\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_S\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} is not in general the identity function, but is (necessarily) an idempotent on the set of matrices over S. We will see that this idempotent, in some sense, measures the degree to which the base semiring S is not boolean.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The idempotent {\operatorname {\underline {mat}}_\mathbb {B}\circ \operatorname {\underline {rel}}_\mathbb {B}\colon \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}\to \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}} is in fact the identity function on matrices over the boolean semiring \mathbb {B}.

fr:title.text: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00IX/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: finite relations as matrices over the booleans › jms-00IX/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We fix a matrix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and compute:
Because \mathbb {B} is the boolean semiring, any scalar s\in S is either 0 or 1. Therefore, we conclude:

Thus we conclude that an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over the booleans is the same thing as a relation from {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} to {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}.

fr:title.text: multiplication of matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: multiplication of matrices

fr:title.text: matrix multiplication in geometry

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix multiplication in geometry

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Although we do not explore it in this course, the viewpoint of matrix multiplication as relational composition generalises to a correct explanation of the role of matrices in geometry as presentations of linear maps between vector spaces in terms of an (uncanonical) choice of basis.

fr:title.text: product of matrices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: product of matrices

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let M be an {\mathopen {}\left (l\times m\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix and let N be a {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix. The product of M and N, written M\otimes N, is the following {\mathopen {}\left (l\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix:

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of matrix products

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The product of matrices is associative and has the identity matrix as neutral element.

fr:title.text: associativity and unit laws of matrix products › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J3/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: associativity and unit laws of matrix products › jms-00J3/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For associativity, fix matrices L\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (k,l\right )\mathclose {}}, M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} to check that {\mathopen {}\left (N\otimes M\right )\mathclose {}}\otimes L = N\otimes {\mathopen {}\left (M\otimes L\right )\mathclose {}}. Below we use the associativity of multiplication, commutativity of addition, and distributivity of multiplication over addition in the semiring S:
For one unit law, we fix M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} and recall the definition of the identity matrix to check that M\otimes I^m = M.

Unfolding the definition of I^m_{i,j} as given in Definition , we see that the iterated sum above expands to m-1 copies of 0\odot M_{k,j} for various k \not = i and one copy of 1\odot M_{i,j}. Thus, we conclude that M\otimes I^m_{i,j} = M_{i,j} using the absorption and unit laws for multiplication as well as the unit laws for addition in S.
The other unit law follows in an analogous way.

fr:title.text: matrix product is relational composition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Under the correspondence between boolean matrices and finite relations (§ , Lemma ), matrix products of boolean matrices correspond to relational composites. In particular, given M\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (l,m\right )\mathclose {}} and N\in \mathrm {Mat}_{\mathbb {B}}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}}, we have:

fr:title.text: matrix product is relational composition › forest://jonmsterling/jms-00J2/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix product is relational composition › jms-00J2/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We use the fact that the additive operation of the boolean semiring is disjunction and the multiplicative operation is conjunction:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: related

fr:title.text: matrix over a semiring

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: matrix over a semiring

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For natural numbers m and n, an {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrix over a semiring {\mathopen {}\left (S,0,\oplus ,1,\odot \right )\mathclose {}} is given by entries M_{i,j}\in S for all i\in {\mathopen {}\left [m\right ]\mathclose {}} and j\in {\mathopen {}\left [n\right ]\mathclose {}}. We will write \mathrm {Mat}_{S}{\mathopen {}\left (m,n\right )\mathclose {}} for the set of {\mathopen {}\left (m\times n\right )\mathclose {}}-matrices.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: relation

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A (binary) relation R from a set A to a set B, written R\colon A\mathbin {\nrightarrow } B or R\in \mathrm {Rel}{\mathopen {}\left (A,B\right )\mathclose {}} is defined to be a subset R\subseteq A\times B. We shall typically write a\mathrel {R}b for {\mathopen {}\left (a,b\right )\mathclose {}}\in R. More generally, a relation between multiple sets {\mathopen {}\left (A_i\right )\mathclose {}}_{i\in I} is defined to be a subset of the cartesian product \prod _{i\in I} A_i.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: contributions
